summary: A randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial (the ViDA study) co-authored by Kay-Tee Khaw, which concludes that monthly high-dose vitamin D supplementation does not prevent falls or non-vertebral fractures in healthy, ambulatory older adults.

principles.statement: Large monthly bolus doses of vitamin D do not prevent falls or fractures in healthy, ambulatory adults.
principles.rationale: Results from a large-scale randomized controlled trial showed that administering 100,000 IU of colecalciferol monthly over several years yielded no significant reduction in the risk of falls or fractures compared to a placebo.
principles.source_quote: High-dose bolus vitamin D supplementation of 100 000 IU colecalciferol monthly over 2·5–4·2 years did not prevent falls or fractures in this healthy, ambulatory, adult population.

signature_quotes.text: High-dose bolus vitamin D supplementation of 100 000 IU colecalciferol monthly over 2·5–4·2 years did not prevent falls or fractures in this healthy, ambulatory, adult population.
signature_quotes.context: Concluding the findings of the ViDA trial regarding secondary and post-hoc outcomes.

signature_quotes.text: These findings, taken in conjunction with results from other trials, suggest that large monthly bolus doses of vitamin D do not confer overall benefit either in frail elderly people or in a healthy, ambulatory, general population.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the broader implications of the trial's evidence on public health practices.

anti_patterns.description: Relying on large, intermittent (monthly) bolus doses of vitamin D to prevent falls and fractures in the general population.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Clinical trial data demonstrates that this specific dosing strategy fails to confer overall musculoskeletal benefits or reduce fall/fracture risks in either frail elderly or healthy ambulatory populations.
